components.deltaSummary: Navius Accordion is structurally complete (Root/Item/Header/Trigger + Content) with solid wrapping roving-focus keyboard support that already matches Base UI. The gap is contract-level, not feature-level. Three blockers: (1) the single data-state=\"open|closed\" string must become Base's discrete presence-booleans - data-open on Item/Header/Panel and data-panel-open on Trigger; (2) the Root value model must be re-architected from Type=single/multiple + Value/Values to Base's array-only Value/DefaultValue + Multiple + unified ValueChanged; (3) Content must be renamed to Panel. components.deltaSummary: Navius AlertDialog is functionally complete and well-structured but named/shaped to the Radix spec. To hit Base UI 1:1: rename Overlay->Backdrop and Content->Popup; swap the single data-state="open|closed" contract for Base UI's discrete booleans (data-open/data-closed on Backdrop+Popup, data-popup-open on Trigger) and add data-starting-style/data-ending-style for animations; add the missing Viewport and Close parts plus nested-dialog states (data-nested, data-nested-dialog-open, --nested-dialogs). Navius's Action/Cancel parts have no Base UI equivalent and must be reconciled. Prop-level work: Root gains onOpenChangeComplete + handle/createHandle/triggerId/payload + actionsRef; Trigger/Close gain nativeButton; Popup moves from OnOpen/CloseAutoFocus events to initialFocus/finalFocus props (and Base UI defaults initial focus to the Popup, not the Cancel control); Portal ForceMount renames to keepMounted. Keyboard behavior (Escape, Tab trap, Enter/Space) already matches.
components.notes: Behavioral parity is already strong: outside-click never dismisses (CloseOnOutside=false), Escape routed via document-level dismiss layer with cancelable hook, scroll lock, focus trap, focus restore — all match Base UI's alert-dialog semantics. The two real semantic gaps vs Base UI are (1) animation lifecycle states (starting/ending-style) which need engine support beyond ForceMount, and (2) the Action/Cancel vs Close divergence — Base UI's alert dialog ships only a generic Close, while Navius encodes APG confirm/cancel roles as first-class parts. Confirm whether the rewrite should preserve Action/Cancel as Navius-specific conveniences or strictly mirror Base UI (Close only).

components.deltaSummary: Base UI Autocomplete is a ~19-part compound (Root, Value, Input, Trigger, Icon, Clear, Status, Portal, Backdrop, Positioner, Popup, Arrow, List, Row, Empty, Collection, Group, GroupLabel, Item, Separator). Navius has only a monolithic single-file NaviusCombobox (root div + input + ul + li) with no context object, no Portal/Positioner/Popup/Arrow/Clear/Trigger/Status/Empty/Group parts. Behaviorally the core (filtering, aria-activedescendant virtual highlight, anchored list via reused Positioner interop, outside-dismiss) exists, so it is partial not missing. Reaching 1:1 requires a full decomposition into a separate Autocomplete compound with a Context cascade, swapping the single data-state and data-active for Base UI discrete booleans (data-open/closed, data-highlighted/selected, data-starting/ending-style, data-side/align, data-empty, field states), adding controlled+uncontrolled open/value, generic items, pluggable filter, mode/auto-highlight props, and the missing keyboard nav (Home/End/PageUp/PageDown) plus CSS anchor variables. This is effectively a ground-up rewrite, not a rename pass.
components.notes: Base UI deliberately splits Autocomplete (free-text input that filters a list, value is the typed string) from Combobox (selection of a value, possibly multi). The existing NaviusCombobox is really an Autocomplete by Base UI's taxonomy, so a clean re-target should create NaviusAutocomplete here and let a future Combobox be its own thing. WebFetch returned a slightly noisy part list (e.g. InputGroup) — the authoritative anatomy above is the standard Base UI set; verify Row/Collection naming against the live MDX during implementation. The Positioner/DismissableLayer JS interop is already reusable, which lowers the positioning cost.

components.deltaSummary: Base UI Button is a single headless root component — no Portal/Positioner/Popup parts — whose contract is: a data-disabled boolean, and props focusableWhenDisabled, nativeButton, and render (element swap). Navius has NO headless Button primitive in the brain; only a styled helm ZitsButton (pure <button> with Tailwind Variant/Size, a Type param, Disabled, OnClick, and unmatched-attribute splatting). To reach 1:1, add a headless NaviusButton primitive providing data-disabled, focusableWhenDisabled (aria-disabled + focusable-when-disabled semantics), nativeButton=false support (role=button + Enter/Space keyboard activation), and an AsChild/render element swap; then re-base ZitsButton on it while keeping Variant/Size as helm-only styling. There are no CSS variables and only the one data attribute, so this is among the smaller component deltas.
components.notes: Base UI Button has no CSS variables and only data-disabled. Variant/Size are shadcn styling that has no Base UI analogue and should stay in helm.

components.deltaSummary: Navius Checkbox is functionally close (tri-state, controlled/uncontrolled, bubble input for form submission, Indicator that mounts only when checked/indeterminate) but does not match Base UI's contract. Two main gaps: (1) the data-attribute model — Navius emits a single data-state="checked|unchecked|indeterminate" whereas Base UI uses DISCRETE booleans data-checked/data-unchecked/data-indeterminate plus data-readonly/data-required and a full Field state set (data-valid/invalid/dirty/touched/filled/focused), and the Indicator lacks data-starting-style/data-ending-style. (2) missing props: readOnly, parent (group roll-up), uncheckedValue, form, id, inputRef, nativeButton, and ForceMount should be renamed keepMounted. Anatomy itself matches (Root + Indicator, no Portal/Positioner/Popup needed). Render model differs (Navius button role=checkbox vs Base UI span+hidden input) but is behavior-equivalent. The discrete-boolean switch is the blocker; Field integration and parent/group support are the largest items.
components.notes: Base UI doc lists no CSS variables and no extra keyboard interactions for Checkbox. Navius indeterminate is modeled as nullable bool (CheckedState=null); Base UI models it as a separate indeterminate prop layered over checked — a 1:1 rewrite should expose both. Field-context data states are only achievable once a Navius Field.Root cascading context exists; if Field is out of scope for this pass, defer that work item.

components.deltaSummary: Navius has no Checkbox Group at all — only a standalone tri-state NaviusCheckbox. Base UI's Checkbox Group is a single Root part (<div role=group>) that coordinates child Checkbox.Root elements by name via value/defaultValue/onValueChange/allValues/disabled/name, supports a `parent` select-all checkbox with automatic indeterminate state, exposes data-disabled plus Field states (touched/dirty/valid/invalid/filled/focused), and has no CSS vars or group-specific keyboard contract. Reaching 1:1 requires building NaviusCheckboxGroup + a group context, making NaviusCheckbox group-aware (derive state from group value[]), adding the `parent` prop, and migrating the child from single data-state to discrete data-checked/data-unchecked/data-indeterminate booleans.

components.deltaSummary: Navius Combobox is a single-file monolith (string-only, single-select, no Context.cs) that covers the APG editable-combobox core: input[role=combobox] with aria-activedescendant, live filtering, ArrowUp/Down/Enter/Escape/Tab, JS-interop positioning + dismissable layer. The single data-state must become discrete data-open/data-closed plus per-part discretes (data-popup-open, data-pressed, data-highlighted, data-selected, data-disabled, data-empty, data-placeholder, data-side/align, data-starting/ending-style, data-multiple, data-readonly). Big missing capabilities: typed generic items, multi-select with chips, controllable open/inputValue, grouping, grid mode, virtualization, form integration, and the five CSS vars. This is a near-total rewrite.

components.deltaSummary:
Navius Form is functionally close but architecturally and API-shaped differently from Base UI. Base UI Form is a single errors-driven <form> with props errors, onClearErrors, onFormSubmit(values, details), validationMode, and actionsRef, and NO data-* attributes; the Field parts are a SEPARATE component. Navius already has solid internals to build on: FormContext registry, FieldContext validity merge (consumer + native ValidityState via interop), FormData snapshot, focus-first-invalid, and aria-describedby message wiring.

components.deltaSummary: Base UI Input is a single-part, field-aware native <input> with controlled/uncontrolled value (value/defaultValue/onValueChange) and 7 discrete data-* boolean states (disabled, valid, invalid, dirty, touched, filled, focused) wired into Field.Root. Navius has NO Input primitive in the brain — only a styled helm passthrough that captures unmatched attributes and applies Tailwind classes, with no value binding, no change events, no field integration, and no data-state contract. Reaching 1:1 requires building the primitive from scratch: native input + binding + the 7 discrete state attributes + Field context integration. No CSS variables or special keyboard handling are required (native input). Field.Root dependency is the main external blocker since that primitive is also missing.

Standalone fallback (focused/filled/dirty/touched tracked locally) keeps the component usable without Field. Keyboard parity is free via native input.

components.deltaSummary: Navius DropdownMenu is a full Radix-shaped menu (Root/Trigger/Portal/Content/Item/CheckboxItem/RadioGroup/RadioItem/ItemIndicator/Label/Separator/Group/Arrow/Sub/SubTrigger/SubContent) and is functionally close, but the structure and contract differ from Base UI in several systematic ways. The second systematic change is the data-attribute model: Navius emits a single data-state token (open|closed, checked|unchecked|indeterminate) everywhere, while Base UI uses discrete booleans (data-open/data-closed, data-checked/data-unchecked) plus animation states (data-starting-style/data-ending-style), data-pressed on Trigger, and data-popup-open on SubmenuTrigger. Props need Base UI naming (OnOpenChange with eventDetails, OnCheckedChange, OnValueChange), Trigger needs openOnHover/delay, items need closeOnClick, and positioning props move onto a new Positioner. CSS vars must rename to --anchor-*/--available-*/--transform-origin. data-highlighted, data-disabled, and most keyboard handling already align.

components.deltaSummary: Meter is entirely MISSING from Navius — no files exist. It must be built from scratch as a 5-part family (Root, Track, Indicator, Value, Label), using the existing Progress component as the structural template (cascading Context, marker attributes, attribute passthrough). Key divergences from Progress: role=\"meter\" not \"progressbar\"; value is required/determinate (no indeterminate/null path); NEW Min prop with min-aware fraction math; NEW Track part; Intl-style Format + Locale number formatting; a Value part with a (formattedValue, value) render function; two-arg GetAriaValueText. Critically, Base UI Meter documents NO data-* attributes, NO CSS variables, and NO keyboard interactions, so the Progress data-state contract must NOT be carried over.
components.notes: Base UI Meter is the simplest of the value-readout components: static, non-interactive, no state objects (all part state types are empty), no data attributes, no CSS vars, no keyboard. Effort is M (not S) only because of the formatting plumbing (Format/Locale via Intl.NumberFormat equivalent in .NET) and the Value render-function part; the structural scaffolding is near-identical to Progress. The helm (Zits.Ui) has no Meter styling either — a styled wrapper would be additional follow-up if helm parity is required.

components.deltaSummary: Navius's NavigationMenu is a functional Radix-shaped headless port (Root/List/Item/Trigger/Content/Link/Sub/Viewport/Indicator) but diverges structurally from Base UI. Base UI nests the open content under Portal > Positioner > Popup with the Viewport (and Arrow) INSIDE the Popup, and has no Indicator. Navius instead self-positions Content or teleports into a standalone Viewport, and ships an Indicator. Reaching 1:1 requires adding 6 new parts (Portal, Backdrop, Positioner, Popup, Arrow, Icon), deleting Indicator, and rebuilding the layout so Content renders inside Popup/Viewport. The data contract must move from a single data-state=open|closed (plus data-motion) to Base UI's discrete booleans (data-popup-open, data-pressed, data-open/closed, data-starting-style/ending-style, data-side) and data-activation-direction. Root timing props change (Delay 200->50, CloseDelay 300->50, drop SkipDelayDuration, add OnOpenChangeComplete); positioning props migrate from Content to Positioner with collision props; CSS vars switch to Base UI's --positioner-*/--popup-*/--available-* set. Three eval() sites should be replaced with interop modules.
components.notes: Base UI navigation-menu has NO Indicator part (unlike Radix) - it uses Arrow inside the Popup; Navius's Indicator is the biggest anatomy mismatch. Base UI Root state is {open, nested} and exposes change reasons (trigger-press, trigger-hover, outside-press, list-navigation, focus-out, escape-key, link-press, none) - Navius's NaviusEscape/PointerDownOutside/FocusOutside/InteractOutside cancelable args partially cover this but should be reconciled to Base UI's reason enum. Navius's Sub part maps to Base UI nested Root (Root renders <nav> at top, <div> when nested) - consolidate Sub into a nested Root rather than a separate component if pursuing strict parity. eval() spots: Content line 230 (FocusFirstChildAsync), Viewport line 70 (MirrorScript), Indicator line 67 (PositionScript).

components.deltaSummary: Navius Popover is a Radix-shaped implementation (Root/Trigger/Anchor/Portal/Content/Arrow/Close) and must be restructured to Base UI's anatomy. The biggest break is splitting the monolithic Content into a Positioner (positioning + collision props, data-side/data-align/data-anchor-hidden) and a Popup (focusable panel, data-open/data-closed/starting/ending). New parts are required: Backdrop, Title, Description, Viewport. The single data-state=\"open|closed\" contract must become discrete booleans (data-open/data-closed) plus data-popup-open/data-pressed on the Trigger and starting/ending-style animation markers. Props need remapping: Modal->bool|'trap-focus', new OnOpenChangeComplete, Trigger openOnHover/delay/closeDelay, ArrowPadding/CollisionPadding defaults 5, Sticky as boolean, Flip/AvoidCollisions->collisionAvoidance/collisionBoundary, and the --navius-popper-* CSS vars renamed to the Base UI --anchor-*/--available-*/--popup-*/--positioner-*/--transform-origin set. Anchor part folds into Positioner.anchor.
components.notes: Navius keeps Radix-style cancelable layer events (OnEscapeKeyDown/OnInteractOutside/OnOpenAutoFocus/etc.) that Base UI Popover does not expose; decide whether to retain as extensions or drop for strict 1:1. Base UI also adds detached-handle features (handle/payload/triggerId) for multi-trigger popovers - out of core scope but noted. Positioning engine (Positioner.cs interop) already computes side/align and CSS vars, so the rewrite is mostly re-homing existing logic onto the new part boundary rather than new positioning math.

The hardest item is wiring real field-state (touched/dirty/focused/valid) since Navius currently has no Field integration for radios. components.deltaSummary: Navius ScrollArea is functionally complete (Root/Viewport+Content/Scrollbar/Thumb/Corner with live JS-driven thumb geometry and drag) but follows the older Radix-style contract, not Base UI. Three structural gaps: (1) anatomy — the Content element is baked inside Viewport rather than being a standalone ScrollArea.Content part; (2) data attributes — Navius uses a single data-state=\"visible|hidden\" on Scrollbar/Thumb plus data-type/data-orientation on Root, whereas Base UI uses DISCRETE booleans (data-has-overflow-x/y, data-overflow-{x,y}-{start,end}, data-scrolling, and data-hovering/data-scrolling on the Scrollbar) and exposes no data-state at all; (3) the whole Type/ScrollHideDelay/hover-timer state machine must go — Base UI does scrollbar reveal/hide purely in CSS off data-hovering/data-scrolling. Props differ: drop Root Type/ScrollHideDelay/Orientation, add Root overflowEdgeThreshold (default 0), rename Scrollbar ForceMount->keepMounted. CSS vars are missing: --scroll-area-corner-width/height (Root), --scroll-area-overflow-{x,y}-{start,end} (Viewport), --scroll-area-thumb-width/height (Scrollbar). No keyboard gap (native overflow). Most underlying geometry already exists in ScrollAreaContext, so this is a contract/anatomy re-shaping rather than a rebuild.
components.notes: Files: NaviusScrollArea.razor, NaviusScrollAreaViewport.razor, NaviusScrollAreaScrollbar.razor, NaviusScrollAreaThumb.razor, NaviusScrollAreaCorner.razor, ScrollAreaContext.cs, ScrollAreaPart.cs (all under src/Navius.Primitives/Components/ScrollArea). No helm/Zits.Ui styled wrapper exists for ScrollArea. Base UI's overflow attrs/vars need an overflowEdgeThreshold compare and per-edge start/end detection (scrollLeft/Top==0 vs ==max), which ScrollAreaContext does not currently track but can derive trivially from existing Metrics. Removing the hide-timer simplifies the brain considerably — bulk of the L estimate is rewiring the Scrollbar/Thumb visibility contract from C# state to CSS-consumable discrete attrs without regressing type=hover/scroll demos.

components.deltaSummary: Navius Select is a complete Radix-style headless implementation (Root/Trigger/Value/Icon/Content/Viewport/Item/ItemText/ItemIndicator/Group/Label/Separator/Scroll*Button/Arrow) but follows the Radix contract, not Base UI. Reaching 1:1 requires: (1) anatomy restructure — split the monolithic Content into Portal+Positioner+Popup, add Backdrop, rename Viewport->List, ScrollUp/DownButton->ScrollUp/DownArrow, Label->GroupLabel plus a new field Label; (2) data-attribute model switch from single data-state to discrete booleans (data-open/closed, data-checked, data-popup-open, data-side, data-anchor-hidden, data-starting/ending-style, field states); (3) feature gaps — Multiple selection, ReadOnly/Modal/Form, AlignItemWithTrigger, enter/exit animation lifecycle, field integration; (4) CSS var rename to --anchor-width/--available-height/--transform-origin. Substantial, hence XL.

components.deltaSummary: Navius Separator is already near-1:1 with Base UI: same single-part anatomy, same data-orientation contract (horizontal omits aria-orientation, vertical sets it), no CSS vars, no keyboard. The one real divergence is the Radix-inherited Decorative prop — Base UI has no decorative concept and always renders a semantic separator (role='separator'). Removing Decorative (and its non-semantic branch) is the only substantive change to reach 1:1. Minor optional cleanups: drop/justify the data-navius-separator marker (not in Base UI) and consider a typed orientation enum. Existing inline comments in NaviusSeparator.razor already reference the Base UI/spec logic for aria-orientation and orientation normalization, so the brain logic is sound; only the Decorative escape hatch needs to go.

components.deltaSummary: Anatomy already matches Base UI 1:1 (Root + Thumb), so this is a contract refactor, not a rebuild. The core blocker is the data-attribute model: Navius emits a single data-state=\"checked|unchecked\" on both parts, while Base UI requires discrete booleans data-checked/data-unchecked. Keyboard/focus already correct via the real <button>; no CSS variables in Base UI's spec.
components.notes: Navius hardcodes <button type=button role=switch> + a NaviusBubbleInput checkbox mirror for form submission, which is a sound implementation. Field states (valid/invalid/dirty/touched/filled/focused) are the largest gap and depend on whether a Navius Field/Form primitive exists yet — if not, those six attrs are blocked on that work and should be sequenced after Field is built. data-disabled already uses Base UI's empty-string-when-present convention, so that one attr is already compliant.

components.deltaSummary: Navius ships two disjoint toast systems (a headless compositional Provider/Viewport/Root/Title/Description/Action/Close tree, and a separate ToastService+NaviusToaster imperative queue). Base UI is one integrated system: Provider + Manager (createToastManager/useToastManager) + Portal/Viewport/Positioner/Root/Content/Title/Description/Action/Close/Arrow, with toasts driven by a manager (add/close/update/promise) and rendered via Root.toast. To reach 1:1: (1) add missing parts Portal, Positioner, Content, Arrow; (2) replace the single data-state contract with discrete booleans data-open/data-closed plus data-starting-style/data-ending-style, and add stack/swipe states data-expanded, data-limited, data-swiping, live data-swipe-direction, data-type, and Content's data-behind; (3) implement the stacking layout (CSS vars --toast-index/--toast-offset-y/--toast-height/--toast-frontmost-height, rename swipe vars to --toast-swipe-movement-x/y); (4) add Provider limit + queueing and rename Duration->timeout; (5) fold ToastService into a real Toast.Manager with add/close/update/promise and an enriched ToastItem; (6) move F8->F6. The manager unification and stacking model are the heavy lifts.
components.notes: Base UI Type semantics (success/error/loading -> data-type) are orthogonal to Navius's existing Type=foreground/background, which actually maps to announcement urgency (role=alert/status, aria-live assertive/polite) — keep both concepts but rename to avoid collision. Navius already has solid pausable timer, swipe interop, Esc-close, and live-region announcer parity. Helm styled layer (helm/Zits.Ui/Components/ZitsToast*.razor) consumes data-state and will need updating in lockstep with the discrete-boolean switch. The compositional tree currently renders Viewport as a bare <ol> with no portal/positioner/stacking; that is the biggest structural gap.

contracts.baseUiModel: Base UI exposes ONE composition primitive: the `render` prop, on every part. Two forms: (a) element form `render={<MyButton/>}` — the part clones the passed element, merges its own props onto it, forwards refs; the custom element must forward `ref` and spread all received props onto its DOM node. (b) function form `render={(props, state) => <el {...props}/>}` — consumer manually spreads `props` and reads `state` (e.g. `state.checked`) for state-driven content/tag. Userland reproduces the contract via `useRender({ defaultTagName, render, props, state, ref, enabled, stateAttributesMapping })`. `defaultTagName` is the element when no render given; `state` auto-serializes to `data-*` attrs (overridable via `stateAttributesMapping`); `ref` accepts one or an array, merging internal+external. Typed via `useRender.ComponentProps<'p',State>` and `useRender.ElementProps<'button'>`. Merging is delegated to `mergeProps`: Object.assign RIGHTMOST-WINS for scalars/style; `className` concatenated rightmost-first (`{className:'a'},{className:'b'}` => `'b a'`); event handlers chained rightmost-FIRST, with `event.preventBaseUIHandler()` letting the user handler cancel earlier (internal) synthetic-event handlers (no effect on custom events); `ref` NOT merged (rightmost only); a prop value may itself be a function receiving accumulated props and returning new props. Net: render-as-element, render-as-tag, state exposure, state->data-attr, and ref-merge are all first-class.
contracts.naviusToday: Navius has two seams. (1) Everyday headless seam: each primitive declares `[Parameter(CaptureUnmatchedValues=true)] Attributes` and splats with `@attributes="Attributes"` onto its OWN fixed root element (Slider, Select, Accordion, etc.). This covers styling/attrs/handlers on the element the primitive already renders — but the element/tag is fixed, not swappable. (2) The asChild approximation `NaviusSlot` + `SlotMerge` (ADR-0003): NaviusSlot renders NO element; it takes props via `Attributes`, captures direct splats via `UnmatchedAttributes`, runs `SlotMerge.Combine`, and hands the merged `IReadOnlyDictionary<string,object>` to a typed `RenderFragment<IReadOnlyDictionary<string,object>>` that the consumer MUST splat onto exactly one root via `@attributes`. SlotMerge semantics: class/className normalized to one `class` and concatenated (forwarded then override, left-to-right); `style` merged per CSS property (incoming wins); `on*` handlers COMPOSED via composeEventHandlers — forwarded/child handler runs FIRST then override/parent, with reflection-based composition for parameterless and single-arg matching delegates, falling back to last-wins; all other keys last-wins (UnmatchedAttributes win). NaviusSlot is only wired into NaviusToastClose today. There is no component STATE passed to the child fragment, no state->data-attr mapping, no ref merge, and no public useRender-equivalent helper for userland primitives.
contracts.blazorConstraint: RenderFragment is opaque (ADR-0003): a parent cannot introspect a child fragment, cannot read a child ElementReference before render, and cannot clone/inject props onto an arbitrary element inside it. Consequences: (1) Base UI's element form `render={<el/>}` is NOT generally achievable — there is no clone-with-merged-props. Approximation: invert control — NaviusSlot emits the merged attribute dictionary and the CONSUMER spreads it onto their own single root via `@attributes` (consumer also chooses the tag, covering render-as-different-element). (2) Ref merging is NOT achievable across the opaque boundary — the consumer owns the element so owns the @ref; documented as a deviation. (3) `event.preventBaseUIHandler()` (synthetic-event cancellation of earlier handlers) has no Blazor equivalent across the seam; can only be approximated with an explicit cancel flag on a shared args object, not via the DOM event. (4) A true zero-DOM 1:1 Slot is impossible only in the arbitrary-element sense; NaviusSlot itself adds no wrapper node, so the no-extra-DOM property IS preserved.
delta: Invert the render contract direction: Base UI passes an element IN (render={<el/>}); Navius passes a merged attribute dict OUT to a RenderFragment the consumer splats. Document this as the canonical Base-UI-equivalent and keep the @attributes seam as the everyday path.

Add a STATE channel to the slot: change ChildContent to receive both merged attrs AND a typed component-state object (tuple or record), mirroring render={(props,state)=>...}. Today only attrs are passed — state-driven child rendering (the Switch.Thumb checked/unchecked case) is impossible.

Introduce a state->data-attribute convention (Base UI's useRender stateAttributesMapping): auto-emit data-* keys into the merged dictionary from a state object so CSS/[data-*] selectors work without manual wiring, with an override hook.

Reconcile merge ORDER with mergeProps: Base UI is rightmost-wins, className rightmost-first ('b a'), handlers rightmost-first. SlotMerge is overrides-win (consistent) BUT className is forwarded-first and handlers are forwarded/child-FIRST. For Tailwind cascade, className order is load-bearing — decide and document one order (recommend matching Base UI rightmost-first, or explicitly justify the inversion).

Document the no-equivalent escape hatch: there is no preventBaseUIHandler. If cancellation across the seam is needed, define an explicit cancelable args record; otherwise state plainly that internal handlers cannot be suppressed by the consumer.

Promote NaviusSlot+SlotMerge into a public useRender-equivalent helper so userland can author primitives with the same render/merge/state contract (Base UI ships useRender for exactly this). Today it is an internal one-off used only by NaviusToastClose.

Wire the slot consistently across primitives that need polymorphism, or document that render-as-element is opt-in per-primitive via NaviusSlot while the rest expose only the @attributes seam (fixed tag).

workItems.change: Add typed component-state to NaviusSlot's ChildContent (RenderFragment of (attrs, state) tuple/record) so render-function parity with render={(props,state)=>...} exists.
Add state->data-* attribute mapping (stateAttributesMapping equivalent) feeding the merged dictionary, with an override hook.

Align SlotMerge ordering with mergeProps: pick and document className concatenation order and event-handler invocation order (rightmost-first vs current forwarded-first); add tests pinning the chosen order.
Publish NaviusSlot+SlotMerge as a documented public composition utility (useRender-equivalent) for userland primitives, not an internal helper.

Document the platform deviations in ADR-0003 follow-up: no element-form render={<el/>}, no ref merge across the opaque child, no preventBaseUIHandler; specify the consumer-splat approximation as the supported contract.
Define an optional cancelable args record to approximate preventBaseUIHandler for composed handlers, or explicitly mark cancellation unsupported.
Audit primitives for which need polymorphic tag swapping and wire NaviusSlot (currently only NaviusToastClose); document the @attributes seam as the fixed-tag default for the rest.

contracts.baseUiModel: Base UI exposes DISCRETE, boolean-presence state attributes rather than one multi-valued attribute: `data-open`/`data-closed`, `data-checked`/`data-unchecked`, `data-highlighted`, `data-selected`, `data-disabled`, trigger-side `data-popup-open`, plus positioning `data-side`/`data-align`. There is no single `data-state`. Enter/exit is a TRANSITION model: an element gets `data-starting-style` for the first committed frame when it becomes visible and `data-ending-style` while it is being hidden, so you write `[data-starting-style] { opacity:0 }` / `[data-ending-style] { opacity:0 }` and let a normal CSS `transition` interpolate. Transitions are preferred over @keyframes because they can be cancelled/reversed mid-flight; `data-open`/`data-closed` are still available if you want keyframe animations. `data-instant` marks state changes that must skip animation. Mounting: popups (Dialog/Popover/Tooltip/Menu/Select) UNMOUNT when closed by default — Base UI calls `element.getAnimations()` and waits for them to finish before removing the node, so exit animations run with no extra flag. `keepMounted` (on Portal) keeps the node in the DOM when closed instead. JS/Motion animations must touch `opacity` (even 0.9999) so Base UI can detect completion. CSS vars are un-prefixed: `--available-height`, `--anchor-width`, `--transform-origin`.
contracts.naviusToday: Navius uses the Radix contract: a single multi-valued `data-state` attribute computed by ~26 `DataState =>` getters in context/component files — `open`/`closed` (Popover, Dialog, AlertDialog, Accordion, Collapsible, HoverCard, all Menus, Select, NavMenu), `checked`/`unchecked`/`indeterminate` (Checkbox, Switch, menu checkbox/radio items), `on`/`off` (Toggle, ToggleGroup, Toolbar), `visible`/`hidden` (Tooltip, ScrollArea thumb/scrollbar, NavMenu indicator, PasswordToggle), `loading`/`complete`/`indeterminate` (Progress), `active`/`disabled` (Slider). Alongside it: `data-side`/`data-align` (written by the JS positioner), `data-orientation`, `data-disabled`, `data-highlighted` (roving focus JS), `data-swipe`/`data-swipe-direction`, `data-hide`. Presence is `@if (Open || ForceMount)`: content unmounts when closed, and the `ForceMount` parameter (38 component files) keeps it rendered with `data-state="closed"` + the `hidden` attribute so CSS exit animations can play. Animation is tailwindcss-animate keyframes gated on Tailwind variants — `data-[state=open]:animate-in data-[state=closed]:animate-out` plus `fade-*`/`zoom-*`/`slide-in-from-*` and `data-[side=*]:` — 52 occurrences across 34 helm components. Collapse height/width animations read `--navius-{prefix}-content-height/width` written by `createSizeObserver` (scrollHeight/scrollWidth via ResizeObserver); the positioner writes `--navius-popper-anchor-width/height`, `--navius-popper-available-width/height`, `--navius-popper-transform-origin` plus per-component aliases.
contracts.blazorConstraint: Base UI's automatic exit-animation-then-unmount relies on React's presence handling + a JS `getAnimations()` await; Blazor has no equivalent built-in presence primitive. Approximation: keep the current `@if (Open || keepMounted)` render gate but, on close, emit `data-ending-style` and defer the actual unmount via a JS interop helper that mirrors Base UI's `element.getAnimations()`/`finished` await before calling back into .NET to drop the node (the existing `NaviusJsInterop` + `OnAfterRenderAsync` lifecycle is the natural seam). `data-starting-style` must be added on the first render after mount then removed on the next frame (a double-render / requestAnimationFrame round-trip from JS, since Blazor can't paint-then-mutate synchronously). Discrete attrs themselves are trivial in Razor (`data-open="@(Open ? "" : null)"`).
delta: Replace the single `data-state` attribute with discrete boolean-presence attributes everywhere it is emitted (~83 .razor sites, ~26 `DataState =>` getters). Mapping is per-component, not 1:1: open/closed -> `data-open`/`data-closed`; checked/unchecked/indeterminate -> `data-checked`/`data-unchecked`/`data-indeterminate`; Toggle on/off -> `data-pressed`/(absent); visible/hidden -> `data-open`/`data-closed`; Progress loading/complete/indeterminate and Slider active/disabled need bespoke Base UI equivalents.

Introduce the `data-starting-style` / `data-ending-style` enter/exit model. Add a render path that stamps `data-starting-style` on the frame an element becomes visible (then clears it next frame) and `data-ending-style` while it is closing, replacing the tailwindcss-animate `animate-in`/`animate-out` keyframe approach with cancelable CSS transitions.

Rename `ForceMount` (38 files) to `keepMounted` semantics: with it set the node stays mounted+`data-closed` when closed; without it, add a JS `getAnimations()`-style await so the default unmount still plays an exit animation before the node is removed (Base UI gives exit animations for free on unmount; Navius must wire the wait-for-animation teardown explicitly).
Add a `data-instant` attribute + plumb an 'instant'/skip-animation path (e.g. controlled jumps, reduced-motion).

Decide the CSS-var contract: either rename `--navius-popper-*` to Base UI's `--anchor-width`/`--available-height`/`--transform-origin` (and `--navius-*-content-height` analogues) or keep the navius-prefixed names as documented aliases; update `createPositioner` and `createSizeObserver` accordingly.

Rewrite the 52 helm `data-[state=*]` Tailwind variants across 34 components to the discrete/transition model (`data-[open]:`/`data-[closed]:`/`data-[starting-style]:`/`data-[ending-style]:`), and either drop tailwindcss-animate's `animate-in/out`+`fade/zoom/slide` keyframes in favour of transitions or re-key them onto the new attributes.

Update the Playwright suite: 11 `toHaveAttribute('data-state', ...)` assertions (navius.spec.ts, wave1.spec.ts, ui.spec.ts) become discrete-attribute checks (`toHaveAttribute('data-open','')` / `not.toHaveAttribute(...)`), and presence/visibility assertions tied to unmount timing must account for the new wait-for-exit-animation teardown.

contracts.naviusToday: Navius mirrors the older Radix `@radix-ui/react-form` model, not Base UI. Anatomy: NaviusForm (<form>) + NaviusFormField (<div>, the Field.Root analog, keyed by required Name) + NaviusFormLabel (<label for>) + NaviusFormControl (<input> or render-prop with ControlProps) + NaviusFormMessage (a Radix-style 'message with match' — the only error surface) + NaviusFormValidityState (render-prop) + NaviusFormSubmit. Field state is ONLY data-valid/data-invalid (computed from FieldValidity.IsInvalid || ServerInvalid), emitted on Field, Label and Control — there is NO data-dirty/touched/filled/focused/disabled. FieldValidity (record) carries the 9 ValidityState booleans + Valid, but no error-message strings. Validity comes from two merged sources: consumer top-down (Validity record or convenience Invalid bool -> CustomError) and native ValidityState mirrored via JS interop (createConstraintValidation -> OnValidityChange -> SetNativeValidityAsync). There is NO validationMode, NO validate callback, NO validationDebounceTime — validation is either native-constraint-driven or fully consumer-controlled. Server errors are a per-field ServerInvalid bool (auto-cleared on next edit in SetValueAsync) plus form-level OnClearServerErrors fired before resubmit/reset — there is NO Form-level errors-by-name object. NaviusFormValidityState exposes only the bare FieldValidity, not errors[]/value/initialValue/transitionStatus. NaviusFormMessage is the error component (match key | MatchFn predicate | catch-all | ForceMatch), driving aria-describedby. There is NO Field.Description and NO Fieldset. Submit blocks on FormContext.IsValid and focuses the first invalid field.
contracts.blazorConstraint: Blazor cannot observe DOM focus/blur, dirty, or 'filled' state purely server-side, and has no equivalent of React's per-render derived field state. data-dirty/touched/filled/focused must be sourced from JS: extend the existing NaviusJsInterop constraint-validation engine to also emit focusin/focusout, input (filled), and an initial-value capture, mirrored back through new FieldContext setters — analogous to the existing OnValidityChange bridge. validationDebounceTime is approximated with a System.Threading.Timer / CancellationToken debounce around an async validate callback invoked on the input bridge. Field.Validity's transitionStatus (an animation-lifecycle value from Base UI's React transition hook) has no native Blazor analog; approximate with the existing data-starting-style/data-ending-style presence pattern used elsewhere in Navius, or expose a coarse 'idle/starting/ending' enum rather than the full React status. errors:string[] is added as a real list on FieldContext (validate return values + Form errors-by-name), since Navius today only has boolean flags. The render-prop richer payload is a straightforward C# record expansion — no platform limit.

contracts.baseUiModel: Two separate utility providers. (1) CSPProvider (`@base-ui/react/csp-provider`) — applies a per-request `nonce` to the inline `<style>` and `<script>` *elements* Base UI injects, so they survive a strict Content-Security-Policy. Why it exists: a few components inject inline style/script (`<ScrollArea.Viewport>`, and `<Select.Popup>`/`<Select.List>` when `alignItemWithTrigger` is on, render an inline `<style>` to hide native scrollbars; script tags are opt-in). Under a CSP with `style-src-elem`/`script-src` allowlists, an un-nonced inline element is blocked, so the dev generates a nonce server-side, lists it in the CSP header, and passes the same value to `<CSPProvider nonce={nonce}>`. Props: `nonce` (string) and `disableStyleElements` (boolean, default false) — the escape hatch that suppresses inline-style injection entirely and relies on external stylesheets, avoiding nonces altogether. Notably it does NOT cover inline style *attributes* (`style=\"...\"`), which fall under `style-src-attr` — Base UI deliberately keeps those out of scope. Crucially, Base UI never uses `eval`/`new Function`; everything is real DOM/element rendering, so the only CSP surface is the nonce on injected elements. (2) DirectionProvider (`@base-ui/react`) — `<DirectionProvider direction=\"ltr|rtl\">`, type `TextDirection`, default `'ltr'`; it only cascades reading direction to descendants (incl. via the `useDirection()` hook for portaled content) and does NOT set `dir`/`direction` CSS itself — the app must.
contracts.naviusToday: DirectionProvider: already a faithful match. `NaviusDirectionProvider.razor` is DOM-transparent (renders no element), cascades `Dir` ("ltr"/"rtl", default "ltr") via `[CascadingValue Name=\"NaviusDirection\"]`, and leaves applying the actual `dir` attribute to descendants — same contract as Base UI (cascade only, app sets the attribute). CSP: there is NO CSP/nonce utility in Navius at all (grep for nonce/Csp/ContentSecurityPolicy across src = 0 hits). Worse, the engine has a proper JS-module bridge (`NaviusJsInterop` → `./_content/Navius.Primitives/navius-interop.js`) but four hot paths bypass it and call `JS.InvokeAsync/InvokeVoidAsync(\"eval\", ...)` with interpolated source strings — the exact `unsafe-eval` smell Base UI never has. Sites: NavigationMenu Viewport (NaviusNavigationMenuViewport.razor:70, MirrorScript ResizeObserver/MutationObserver installer), NavigationMenu Indicator (NaviusNavigationMenuIndicator.razor:67, PositionScript size/position vars), NavigationMenu Content (NaviusNavigationMenuContent.razor:230, FocusFirstScript focus-first), and helm ZitsCommand (ZitsCommand.razor:200-206, scrollIntoView on a GUID id). All four interpolate C#-derived values into a string handed to `eval`.
contracts.blazorConstraint: Blazor cannot replicate Base UI's nonce-on-injected-element model literally, because Blazor components don't render their own `<style>`/`<script>` tags the way Base UI does — Navius does its DOM work through `JS.Invoke` into a static, pre-bundled JS module (`navius-interop.js`) served as a static asset, which is already nonce-free and CSP-clean (no inline element, no eval) once the `eval` sites are migrated into it. So the chosen approximation is two-pronged: (a) eliminate the `eval` calls (the real `unsafe-eval` blockers) by moving those snippets into named exports of `navius-interop.js` and calling them via `NaviusJsInterop`; (b) add a thin `NaviusCspProvider` that cascades a `Nonce` string, used to stamp `nonce=\"...\"` on any inline `<style>`/`<script>` Navius (or consumers via slots) emits and to tag the dynamic `import()`/static asset references where the host CSP requires it. Net: parity is achieved by being eval-free + offering nonce plumbing, rather than by mirroring Base UI's per-element nonce injection one-for-one.
delta: Add a new NaviusCspProvider.razor (DOM-transparent, same pattern as NaviusDirectionProvider): a [Parameter] string? Nonce cascaded as [CascadingValue Name="NaviusCspNonce"], plus an optional bool DisableStyleElements (default false) mirroring Base UI's escape hatch.

Add a NaviusCsp ambient service / cascading read so deep components and the JS interop layer can fetch the current nonce without prop-drilling (consumed by NaviusJsInterop when it injects anything inline).

Migrate the 4 eval() sites into named exports of navius-interop.js and call them through NaviusJsInterop — removing all JS.Invoke("eval", ...) usage so the library is safe under a CSP without 'unsafe-eval': NavigationMenuViewport MirrorScript -> a createViewportMirror(slotSelector, idBase) export; NavigationMenuIndicator PositionScript -> a setIndicatorMetrics(size, position) export (pass values as args, not interpolated source); NavigationMenuContent FocusFirstScript -> a focusFirstDescendant(contentId) export; helm ZitsCommand scrollIntoView -> a scrollIntoViewById(id) export (or the trivial JS.InvokeVoidAsync of a static helper).

Because the snippets currently interpolate C# values into source strings, also close the (minor) injection surface by passing IdBase/contentId/id/size/position as typed JS args rather than string concatenation.

Document the CSP contract: Navius requires no 'unsafe-eval' and no 'unsafe-inline' once migrated; any inline style/script Navius emits carries the cascaded Nonce, and inline style *attributes* (style="--var: ...") remain the consumer's responsibility under style-src-attr — same caveat Base UI documents.

Confirm DirectionProvider needs no behavioral change — only naming/doc parity (Navius uses Dir:string; Base UI uses direction:TextDirection). Optionally expose a useDirection-equivalent for portaled content if portals are added.
